distributed_systems_fundamentals.evidence_summary: Student demonstrated Level 5 independent reasoning on the underlying tradeoff concept (availability/confidentiality) before CAP was formally introduced, but could not apply or retain the formal CAP theorem after the Companion explained it — performance dropped to Level 2 and Level 1 for the named concept, and the student self-reported not retaining the acronyms. Trajectory declined once the formal theorem was introduced, though the underlying intuition was already present.
distributed_systems_fundamentals.source_notes: Independently articulates availability/confidentiality tradeoffs with concrete examples (more servers = more attack surface), but does not retain CAP as a formal framework — self-reported forgetting the acronym. Next session should probe whether the student can independently map a distributed system scenario to the C-vs-A choice without being told which theorem applies, and whether they can name the three CAP properties unprompted.

information_security_fundamentals.evidence_summary: Strong upward trajectory from prior score of 0.28: student demonstrated Level 5 independent knowledge of the full CIA triad with concrete implementation examples, Level 4 self-correction on the authentication/authorization distinction, and Level 6 generative synthesis connecting public-key authentication to role-based authorization in a blockchain system. One weak persisting concern: the integrity-as-backup conflation was corrected by the Companion but not reformulated in the student's own words.
information_security_fundamentals.source_notes: Has solid independent working knowledge of CIA triad and correctly applies availability/confidentiality tradeoffs. Self-corrected the auth/authz distinction and extended it independently to a blockchain access-control model. Residual gap: integrity was initially framed as backup/loss-prevention rather than unauthorized-alteration prevention — the Companion corrected this but the student did not reformulate it independently. Next session should open by asking the student to define integrity without scaffolding to verify whether the correction held.

blockchain_fundamentals.evidence_summary: Level 6 evidence from the session's opening exchange — student independently explained hash-chain immutability, PoW cost, and cascading invalidation — and Level 6 again when independently generating the axolotes use case as a novel non-financial application of trustless multi-party record-keeping. The prior session's residual financial-utility misconception was operationally resolved through self-generated demonstration, though not explicitly named as a correction. Trajectory was uniformly strong throughout.
blockchain_fundamentals.source_notes: Owns the core blockchain value proposition — can independently explain structural immutability, PoW cost, and trustless multi-party record-keeping across novel non-financial domains. The prior financial-utility misconception appears behaviorally resolved through spontaneous generation of a non-financial use case. Next session can advance to deeper mechanics (consensus, chain forks, finality) without re-establishing fundamentals.

blockchain_types.evidence_summary: Returned concept where the prior session's targeted test (supply chain with competing manufacturers) was applied independently and correctly, with bilateral reasoning for rejecting public and private options. The consortium term — previously unknown — was recalled and applied correctly. A minor imprecision on private blockchain scope (one person vs. one organization) was noted and corrected but not a structured misconception. Trajectory was positive throughout.
blockchain_types.source_notes: Can independently map use cases to the three-type taxonomy and articulate the reasoning for consortium selection in a competitive multi-party setting. Private blockchain was slightly misdefined as single-person rather than single-organization control — verify this distinction holds in the next session. Ready to extend to more nuanced governance questions: e.g., what happens when a consortium member wants to leave, or how voting rights are structured.

cryptography_introduction.evidence_summary: Student entered with prior working knowledge and demonstrated Level 5 independent reasoning from the session's opening (encryption as data obfuscation requiring a key, symmetric vs. asymmetric distinction) and Level 6 generative reasoning mid-session (independently connecting the directional inversion of asymmetric cryptography to integrity/signing without prompting). Trajectory was uniformly strong with no misconceptions identified for this framing.
cryptography_introduction.source_notes: Arrived with a functional mental model of cryptography as a key-based data-obfuscation tool and independently connected it to both confidentiality and integrity goals. Generated the signing-as-inversion insight without scaffolding and synthesized encryption + hash into a complete document security solution unprompted. Next session can build directly on this foundation toward deeper mechanics — key exchange protocols, certificate authorities, or the formal distinction between encryption and signing — without re-establishing the basics.

hash_tables.evidence_summary: Multiple Level 5 independent moments from the session's opening — student independently identified hashing as the solution before the concept was introduced, correctly executed modular arithmetic unprompted, and enumerated both collision strategies without scaffolding. One Level 4 self-correction on O(n) notation scope. Trajectory was consistently high with no scaffolding dependency observed.
hash_tables.source_notes: Arrived with partial hash knowledge from prior sessions and immediately applied it to the table structure independently. Can execute modular indexing correctly and describe both linear probing and chaining without prompting. Self-corrected on O(n) worst-case scope with substantive explanation. Next session should test procedural execution on a novel collision scenario (e.g., deletion with tombstone flags) to verify the concept holds under edge cases the student has not yet encountered.

distributed_hash_tables.evidence_summary: Trajectory improved from early Level 2 scaffolded responses to Level 5 and Level 6 independent reasoning by mid-session. Student independently identified the load-imbalance weakness of consistent hashing, independently connected DHT to blockchain's node-churn problem, independently critiqued consistent hashing as insufficient for multi-node failure scenarios, and independently described the neighbor-query propagation mechanism for new node discovery — all without prompting.
distributed_hash_tables.source_notes: Demonstrates genuine ownership of the core DHT value proposition — can independently explain why consistent hashing minimizes migration cost during node churn, identify its load-distribution weakness, and describe decentralized node discovery through neighbor propagation. The early confusion about clockwise directionality resolved quickly. Next session should probe whether the student can independently explain consistent hashing's circle metaphor and virtual nodes without the scaffolding that was present in this session's opening.

solidity_mappings.evidence_summary: Early performance was heavily scaffolded (Level 2): could not recall declaration syntax, did not know the `address` type, required the full pattern to be shown. By mid-to-late session the student independently applied mappings to a new voting contract (Level 5) with two minor errors (capitalization and key selection) corrected after single exchanges. Trajectory improved from Level 2 (early) to Level 5 (late) across the session.
solidity_mappings.source_notes: Understands mappings conceptually (dictionary analogy) and can apply the pattern to a new scenario independently, but cannot yet recall declaration syntax without seeing an example first — attempted incorrect syntax (wrong type, wrong arrow direction) before being shown the correct form. The `msg.sender` vs. `candidato` key confusion in the voting exercise suggests the concept of 'which entity is the index' is not yet stable. Next session should present a new mapping scenario (e.g., an allowance system or an NFT owner registry) and ask the student to write the full declaration and an update function from scratch, without showing an example first.

ethereum_platform.evidence_summary: One Level 5 moment (high-level automation framing) and one knowledge-transfer correction (full vs. light nodes) at the opening, but structural mechanics — why distributed execution is necessary and what gas does — remained unresolved after scaffolding. Trajectory was flat-to-declining once structural depth was required.
ethereum_platform.source_notes: Grasps the surface value proposition of Ethereum (code automation, eliminating intermediaries) independently, but cannot yet explain why contracts must be executed across multiple nodes or articulate the efficiency-vs-decentralization trade-off that motivates this design. The student self-reported this as their main confusion point. Next session should open by asking the student to explain, in their own words, why a single trusted server couldn't just run the smart contract — to probe whether the distributed-execution rationale has been internalized.

smart_contracts.evidence_summary: Level 5 recall of the term and a strong Level 6 generative example (multi-party real estate disbursement with automatic title transfer) at first exposure, with no scaffolding required for either moment. No further depth was probed, leaving the trajectory flat after the strong opening.
smart_contracts.source_notes: Independently generated a well-structured multi-party conditional execution example (real estate transaction with automatic payment and title transfer) that captures the core smart contract logic without prompting. The concept of condition-triggered, intermediary-free code execution appears genuinely understood at the conceptual level. Next session should test whether this understanding extends to edge cases: what happens if a condition is never met, or if the code has a bug — to probe whether the student understands the immutability and trustlessness constraints that make smart contract design non-trivial.

solidity_basics.evidence_summary: Level 5 recall of Solidity's name, followed by two minimal Level 2 correct guesses (count increments; transactions queue) that were directly readable from the provided code example. No independent reasoning about Solidity mechanics or the EVM execution model was demonstrated. Trajectory was flat throughout.
solidity_basics.source_notes: Can name Solidity as Ethereum's language and correctly read a simple counter contract, but has not demonstrated any reasoning about Solidity's type system, function visibility, state persistence, or the execution model. The sequential transaction answer ('fila?') was a tentative guess, not confident reasoning. Next session should walk through a slightly more complex contract (e.g., one with a conditional and a state variable that tracks multiple callers) and ask the student to predict behavior before running it, to develop active rather than passive code-reading.

blockchain_dev_tools.evidence_summary: Single Level 6 evidence moment late in the session: student independently described their own migration from Remix to Hardhat, articulating the specific friction point (manual deploy cycle) and the concrete benefit of local testing — going substantially beyond the Companion's one-sentence descriptions of each tool.
blockchain_dev_tools.source_notes: Has practical hands-on experience with both Remix and Hardhat and independently articulated the trade-off that drives tool selection (rapid prototyping vs. automated local testing). This is real operational knowledge, not surface-level recall. Coverage did not extend to test scripting, deployment configuration, or network simulation. Next session should build on this practical foundation by asking the student to describe how they currently structure a Hardhat test, to assess depth beyond the deploy-vs-test distinction.

crypto_wallets.evidence_summary: Multiple Level 5 independent moments from the session's opening (private key as access mechanism, Bitcoin on-network not on-device, custodial risk extension to data leaks and fund loss) with one Level 4 self-correction at an impasse on seed phrase derivation. Trajectory was strongly positive throughout, with a single Level 2 dip on unfamiliar vocabulary ('custodial') that resolved immediately once the term was grounded.
crypto_wallets.source_notes: Arrived with strong prerequisite knowledge (asymmetric cryptography) and independently reasoned through wallet mechanics, custodial risk, and dApp integration choice without scaffolding. The seed-phrase-as-private-key equivalence was partially corrected — student generated the right directional model ('seed + identifier') but did not verbalize the one-to-many derivation concept independently. Next session should open by asking the student to explain what a seed phrase is and how it differs from a private key in their own words, to verify whether the HD wallet concept was retained.

sequence_diagrams.evidence_summary: The Companion presented one example and asked the student to produce a simple diagram; the student explicitly opted out ('Vamos pular isso'). No engagement with the concept at any level was observed.
sequence_diagrams.source_notes: Concept was presented with one worked example but the student declined the practice exercise entirely. No understanding can be inferred. Next session must treat this as completely unlearned and should start with a very simple worked example before asking the student to produce one — the login scenario the student skipped would be an appropriate starting point.

hash_functions.evidence_summary: Started at Level 0 (genuine knowledge gap — could not identify hash as an alternative to encryption), but after a single analogical prompt immediately recalled and independently applied the concept (Level 3 → Level 5), then demonstrated Level 5 on the salt/collision problem and Level 6 in the final document synthesis. One weak Level 2 error (partial hash comparison) was self-corrected with explanation after one follow-up question. Strongly improving trajectory across the second half.
hash_functions.source_notes: Knows hash functions as one-way transformations and correctly applies them to password storage, file integrity, and multi-concept security architectures. Independently anticipated the salt concept and correctly described the avalanche effect by name-and-behavior (though not the formal term). The concept was not spontaneously accessible at session start — required an analogical trigger to activate prior knowledge. Next session should open with an unprompted question (e.g., 'how would you store passwords securely?') to verify the concept is now independently retrievable without the analogy.

symmetric_cryptography.evidence_summary: Student demonstrated Level 5 independent reasoning on symmetric cryptography twice — correctly identifying the shared-key model and independently naming the key distribution problem as its core weakness — without any scaffolding. Coverage was brief as the session moved toward asymmetric, but all evidence moments were independent and correct. No misconceptions identified.
symmetric_cryptography.source_notes: Can independently articulate the symmetric model (shared key, both parties must possess it) and correctly identifies key distribution as its fundamental weakness. Applied this knowledge to reframe a novel 'two private keys' proposal as equivalent to the symmetric problem. Coverage was limited to the core concept — mechanics like block vs. stream ciphers, key sizes, or specific algorithms were not explored. Next session should probe whether the student can describe a concrete scenario where symmetric cryptography is appropriate and why, to verify understanding extends beyond the weakness framing.

asymmetric_cryptography.evidence_summary: Early misconception (sender uses private key to encrypt for confidentiality, rather than recipient's public key) was partially resolved — the student demonstrated correct directional usage in subsequent exchanges but did not verbally articulate why the earlier reasoning was wrong. Later evidence was Level 5 (digital signing as intentional directional inversion) and Level 6 (combined asymmetric + hash solution). Trajectory improved strongly after the early misconception.
asymmetric_cryptography.source_notes: Understands the two-directional logic of asymmetric cryptography and correctly distinguishes encryption (recipient's public key encrypts, recipient's private key decrypts) from signing (sender's private key signs, sender's public key verifies) as inverse operations. The initial key-direction confusion was corrected in behavior but not explicitly verbalized — the student said 'meu exemplo faz bastante sentido' even after being corrected, suggesting partial residual resistance. Next session should open by asking the student to walk through both directions (encrypt for confidentiality, sign for authenticity) unprompted to verify the distinction is now stable and accessible.

digital_signatures.evidence_summary: Session ended after two student turns — the only substantive response ('única e não fungível') was vague analogical language that required immediate redirection (Level 2 at best), and the closing diagnostic question about which key to use for signing went unanswered (Level 0). No independent reasoning about the cryptographic mechanism was observed.
digital_signatures.source_notes: Concept was introduced via physical signature analogy but the session ended before the student engaged with the cryptographic mechanism at all. The 'não fungível' response suggests loose borrowing from blockchain vocabulary rather than reasoning about digital signatures. Next session must resume from the unanswered question: ask the student which key (public or private) they would use to sign a document and why, before progressing to the hash-then-sign structure. The student's prior working knowledge of asymmetric key directions (from the asymmetric_cryptography profile) may activate quickly once the question is posed — the prerequisite knowledge is there but the connection to signing has not been made yet.

evm_and_virtualization.evidence_summary: Level 5 independent performance across three distinct aspects — distributed execution rationale, virtual machine as hardware standardization, and gas as execution cost — with one Level 6 moment (student independently connected gas estimation to their personal MetaMask experience without prompting). Trajectory was consistently strong, with the prior session's flagged confusion point (why distributed execution is necessary) resolved independently at the session's opening.
evm_and_virtualization.source_notes: Independently resolved the distributed-execution rationale that was flagged as a confusion point in the prior session, correctly identified virtual machine standardization as the cross-platform determinism solution, and connected gas to the MetaMask cost-estimation UI from personal experience. Concept was first exposure but performed at Level 5–6 across multiple exchanges. Next session should probe whether the student can explain the EVM's role without prompting and articulate why determinism is essential for consensus — the gas-incentive structure and its connection to miner economics has not yet been explored.

smart_contract_security.evidence_summary: Opened with Level 5 independent identification of the reentrancy vulnerability pattern (state not zeroed before funds sent allows infinite drain) and closed with Level 5 self-generated explanation of the attack in plain language. Mid-session mechanism details required scaffolding (Level 2–3): the student initially misattributed the vulnerability to distributed network architecture rather than execution flow, which was corrected by the Companion. The reentrancy guard (mutex pattern) was reached after one narrowing question (Level 3).
smart_contract_security.source_notes: Independently identified the core reentrancy vulnerability and produced a correct plain-language explanation at the session's close. The mechanism — contract-to-contract recursive callback before state update — required scaffolding to reach but was subsequently articulated in the student's own words. The Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern was named by the Companion, not the student. Next session should present a new vulnerable contract snippet and ask the student to identify the vulnerability and propose the fix without scaffolding, to test whether the pattern has been internalized rather than just recognized in the explained example.

software_testing_and_quality.evidence_summary: Level 5 conceptual performance on test structure (independently identified negative-path and positive-path testing priorities in correct order without the category names) but Level 2 procedural performance (could not write test setup, made two structural errors in the attempted function). Student explicitly disclosed that all prior testing was AI-generated, confirming genuine first exposure at the implementation level. Trajectory was flat.
software_testing_and_quality.source_notes: Grasps the conceptual logic of test design — correctly identified that a voting test should first verify rejection of invalid inputs, then verify state updates — but cannot independently write a Foundry test: missed the `test_` naming convention, accessed the mapping directly instead of through the contract instance, and did not know how to instantiate a contract for testing. All prior testing experience was AI-assisted. Next session should ask the student to write a complete test from scratch for a simple contract (e.g., the `adicionarTokens` function from this session) without showing an example first, to establish a baseline for procedural execution.

computer_networks_fundamentals.evidence_summary: Uneven coverage: early DNS understanding was scaffolded and incomplete (centralized framing required correction; DNS hierarchy was accepted but not reformulated independently), while packet-switching rationale and routing propagation were explained independently and correctly at Level 5. Student explicitly self-identified a knowledge gap in protocol layers (OSI model). Trajectory was flat-to-improving, with stronger independent performance in the second half of the section.
computer_networks_fundamentals.source_notes: Can independently reason about why packet-switching exists (resilience and parallelism) and how routing tables propagate through neighbor queries, but does not have an independent model of DNS resolution or the TCP/IP stack. The centralized-DNS starting assumption suggests incomplete mental model of how distributed naming works. Next session should open by asking the student to explain what happens between typing a URL and receiving a response — without any prompting — to assess which parts of the flow are independently retrievable.

p2p_networks.evidence_summary: Consistently strong Level 5 and Level 6 evidence throughout the section. Student independently reconstructed BitTorrent's core mechanism from earlier DHT content, contributed an independent insight about availability-driven latency (not prompted), and produced a closing synthesis that extended the Companion's 'resilience' framing into a four-part failure-mode taxonomy. Trajectory was uniformly strong, benefiting from cumulative context built during the DHT discussion.
p2p_networks.source_notes: Owns the P2P value proposition — can independently articulate why decentralized file distribution outperforms central servers and connect it to blockchain's infrastructure requirements. Generated a four-part resilience taxonomy (malicious actors, bad actors, node departure, node arrival) independently. A loose Tor-P2P association was noted but is not a structural misconception — it reflects incomplete boundary-drawing between network types rather than an incorrect model. Next session should distinguish P2P from client-server and from Tor/overlay networks to sharpen the student's mental model of when each architecture applies.
